In the wake of Kratos Defense & Security Solutions Inc. (KTOS) landing a large government contract for hypersonic missile testing, James Rogers named other companies that can benefit from this weapons development trend.

data.title: Dow sees worst start to a year since 2016 as Fed rate-cut hopes fade for 2025
data.description:
By Christine Idzelis

U.S. stocks sold off sharply Friday

U.S. stocks are struggling in 2025, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average off to its worst start to a year since 2016, as markets on Friday reflected a greater chance of the Federal Reserve pausing interest-rate cuts at least until May.

The Dow DJIA dropped almost 700 points Friday, booking a back-to-back weekly decline that left it down 1.4% so far in January. That marked the index's worst performance over the first six trading days of a year since 2016, when it slumped 5.9%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Equities broadly fell under pressure Friday after a hotter-than-forecast jobs report sparked a jump in Treasury yields. The report - which showed the U.S. economy added more jobs in December than Wall Street expected and a decline in the unemployment rate - spurred traders in the federal-funds futures market to price in a greater probability that the Fed will maintain its benchmark rate at the current level at its next two policy meetings in January and March.

"The fact that employment is going so strong despite rates where they are will continue to fuel talk of higher neutral rates," said Neil Dutta, head of economic research at Renaissance Macro Research, in a note Friday. "Markets are responding in kind with a broad tightening of financial-market conditions - rates up, dollar up, stocks down - and now seeing just one rate cut in 2025."

All three major U.S. stock indexes closed sharply lower Friday, with the Dow and the technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite COMP each dropping 1.6% and the S&P 500 SPX slumping 1.5%.

The S&P 500 saw nearly all its sectors end in the red on Friday, with the index's year-to-date loss of 0.9% marking its worst performance over the first six trading days of a year since 2022, according to Dow Jones Market Data. The selloff has hammered the S&P 500's rate-sensitive real-estate sector XX:SP500.60, which saw a 2.5% drop Friday that deepened its loss so far this year to 3.8%.

Rates in the bond market climbed Friday, with the yield on the 10-year Treasury note BX:TMUBMUSD10Y jumping to 4.772%, its highest level since Nov. 1, 2023, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

While the U.S. jobs report released on Friday is positive from an economic standpoint, the employment data "solidified" the notion that, at least in near term, the Fed won't be cutting rates, said Kevin Gordon, senior investment strategist at Charles Schwab, in a phone interview.

Traders in the fed-funds futures market on Friday priced in a near-certain probability that the Fed will decide at its policy meeting in late January to keep its benchmark rate at the current target range of 4.25% to 4.5%, according to the CME FedWatch Tool. Fed-funds futures reflected a roughly 75% chance that the Fed will remain on pause in March, up from a 59% probability priced Friday morning before the U.S. jobs report was released.

After the stock market's close on Friday, the CME data indicated fed-funds-futures traders saw a 55.6% chance that the Fed could cut its policy rate by a quarter of a percentage point in May.

Despite Friday's stock-market slump, "as long as the Fed is on hold because the economy stays together and the labor market is resilient, I think that's a good thing for risk assets," said Gordon.

Friday's employment report showed the U.S. economy added 256,000 jobs in December, while the unemployment rate fell slightly to 4.1%.

Negative market reaction driven by inflation concerns may have been "a little bit overdone" on Friday, as the employment report showed wage growth slowed slightly in December, according to Gordon.

But he cautioned that if inflation readings next week turn out to be hotter than expected, there's more risk of a "protracted drawdown" in the U.S. stock market.

Meanwhile, most of the S&P 500's 11 sectors are down so far in 2025.

After real estate, consumer staples XX:SP500.30 and financials XX:SP500.40 have the next-biggest losses, with each down more than 2% so far this month, according to FactSet data. Information technology XX:SP500.45, the S&P 500's largest sector by far, ended Friday with the fourth-largest year-to-date losses, down 1.7%.

Meanwhile, Wall Street's so-called fear gauge, the Cboe Volatility Index VIX, is up in 2025. The index, which trades under the ticker symbol VIX, has climbed 12.6% this month to end Friday at 19.54.

"We believe investors need to be more discerning as volatility may stay elevated," said analysts at Citigroup in a research note Friday.

-Christine Idzelis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Bitcoin Sentiment Plummets To Neutral: Reversal Signal?
data.description:
Data shows the Bitcoin Fear & Greed Index has declined to a neutral level recently. Here’s what this could imply for the cryptocurrency’s price.

Bitcoin Fear & Greed Index Is Now Pointing At ‘Neutral’

The “Fear & Greed Index” refers to an indicator created by Alternative that tells us about the average sentiment present among the traders in the Bitcoin and wider cryptocurrency markets.

This metric uses the data of the following five factors to calculate its value: volatility, trading volume, social media sentiment, market cap dominance, and Google Trends.

When the indicator has a value greater than 53, it means the investors as a whole share a sentiment of greed. On the other hand, it being under 47 implies the dominance of fear in the market. All values in the range lying between these cutoffs correspond to a net neutral mentality.

Besides these three main sentiments, there are also two ‘extreme’ ones called the extreme fear and extreme greed. The former occurs at or above 75 and the latter at or under 25.

Now, here is how the Bitcoin Fear & Greed Index is looking at the moment:

As displayed above, the indicator has a value of 50, which implies the overall sentiment in the market is exactly in the balance. This is a stark change from yesterday, when the index was sitting at 69.

The below chart shows how the Fear & Greed Index has seen its value change over the past twelve months.

Just a few days ago, the indicator’s value was even higher at 78, meaning that the market held a majority sentiment of extreme greed. The sharp drop in the investor mentality since then is a result of the Bitcoin recovery rally fizzling out and turning into a price crash.

This is the first time since the first half of October that the index has dropped into the neutral territory. Between then and now, the market only carried an optimistic atmosphere as the asset’s price was following an upwards trajectory.

With this reset, though, it appears the investors are now unsure about the future of the cryptocurrency. If history is anything to go by, this may not actually be a bad thing.

Bitcoin and other digital assets have generally tended to move in a way that goes contrary to the expectations of the crowd; extreme greed is where major tops have occurred, including the one from last year, and extreme fear is where bottoms have taken place.

While the market hasn’t become fearful yet, the fact that there is no longer an excess of hype could still be something that can help the price find a reversal. It now remains to be seen how BTC and the market sentiment would develop in the coming days.

BTC Price

At the time of writing, Bitcoin is floating around $94,200, down almost 4% in the last seven days.

data.title: Usual Protocol to activate ‘revenue switch’ after USD0++ depegs
data.description:
Usual Protocol, a decentralized finance (DeFi) stablecoin issuer, responded to community backlash on Friday, Jan. 11, after its staked stablecoin USD0++ sharply depegged from $1. 

On Jan. 9, USD0++ dropped as low as $0.89 before reaching $0.92, following the introduction of a new floor price mechanism and exit options.

The protocol’s team has now introduced a series of measures, including the early activation of a “revenue switch,” to address user concerns and stabilize the ecosystem.

The revenue switch, starting on Jan. 13, allows Usual Protocol to share its earnings from real-world assets and protocol operations with its community. The team projects approximately $5 million in monthly revenues, translating to an annual percentage return of more than 50% under current conditions. These distributions will occur weekly.

“This initiative aims to highlight the tangible value of USUAL, the balance of its economic model, and the income generated by the protocol,” Usual Protocol posted on X.

The team also confirmed that a “1:1 Early Unstaking” feature will be activated next week, allowing users to redeem their USD0++ at the $1 peg but requiring them to forfeit a portion of their accrued rewards as a penalty. 

USD0++ depegs

Usual Labs had altered the code governing USD0++ earlier on Friday, reducing its redemption value from $0.995 to a new minimum floor price of $0.87. This change blindsided many investors and developers who relied on the assumption that USD0++ could always be redeemed one-to-one for USD0, a stablecoin pegged to the US dollar.

The update introduced dual exit mechanisms to align USD0++ with its vision of a bond-like financial instrument backed by real-world revenue streams. Users now have two options — a “conditional exit” for 1:1 redemption at the $1 peg, requiring forfeiture of accrued rewards, or an “unconditional exit” offering immediate cash-out at a floor price of $0.87, which will gradually rise to $1 over four years.

These changes led to multimillion-dollar liquidations and liquidity shifts on platforms such as Curve Finance and Pendle.

USD0, a stablecoin with a market capitalization of $1.57 billion, is pegged to the US dollar and fully backed by real-world assets such as US Treasury bills. Users can stake USD0 to convert it into USD0++, a bond-like token that locks funds for four years and generates yield in the protocol’s native token, USUAL.

Community backlash

Stani Kulechov, founder of DeFi platform Aave, criticized the update on X, calling it an example of how hardcoded and immutable price feeds can lead to problems.

Michael Egorov, founder of Curve Finance, highlighted the underlying mechanics of USD0++, noting that its backing by 4-year Treasury bills makes a discount likely. He said, “USD0++ should likely have a discount,” adding that the change caught many off guard. “This was unexpected for many, so some protocols hardcoded price oracle for USD0++ to 1.0,” Egorov stated.

Ignas, a DeFi researcher, questioned the governance process behind the update.

“Whitepaper specifies that ‘the DAO reserves the authority to set this price floor, allowing USD0++ holders to exchange their tokens for USD0 at a rate below the standard 1:1 redemption ratio.' Where was the DAO vote? USUALx holders needed to vote on this,” Ignas pointed out.

Cointelegraph reached out to Usual Labs for comments but received no response before publication.

1845 GMT - The number of rigs drilling for oil and gas in Canada rose by 122 this week to 216, recovering from some large declines in December, according to data from oil services company Baker Hughes. Oil rigs were up by 100 at 144 and gas rigs increased by 22 to 72. Winter is typically a strong drilling season in Canada because there is no need to compensate farmers for lost crops on the fields, and many companies use the winter to delineate reservoirs, says Rystad Energy's Susan Bell. The Christmas break could be behind the low count of previous weeks, she adds. Canadian rig counts also drop during the spring thaw when the ground becomes mucky and hard to move around and road bans limiting axle weight on trucks makes it tough to move rigs on roads, Bell adds. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1815 GMT - Constellation Energy's acquisition of Calpine for $16.4 billion is set to create the nation's largest independent power producer, Gabelli Funds' Tim Winter says in a research note. He views the merger favorably, as it comes at an attractive price, should boost Constellation's earnings by $2 a share, and would expand and diversify Constellation's regional presence in the nonregulated markets of Texas, California and the PJM Interconnection, a regional transmission organization operating in 13 mid-Atlantic states and the District of Columbia. Constellation will now be bigger in Texas while having less overall exposure to the PJM region, Winter says. The supply-demand situation in those regions appears very favorable, he says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1755 GMT - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. fell by two this week to 480, and was down by 19 from a year ago, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. The oil rig count has remained below 500 since May of 2024 with producers taking advantage of drilling and well efficiency to raise production to a record level. U.S. crude oil output has been around 13.6 million barrels a day for the past five weeks, according to EIA estimates. Rigs directed at natural gas decreased by three this week to 100, compared with 117 a year ago, Baker Hughes says. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1503 GMT - Crude futures test new multi-month highs after a Reuters report that the U.S. plans to expand sanctions on Russian oil. "I think these reports are being taken more seriously now because we're starting to see China and India back away from actually buying the sanctioned barrels," says Rebecca Babin of CIBC Private Wealth US. Previous buyer reaction to increased sanctions was "yeah, whatever, you're not going to enforce, now they're taking it a bit more seriously," she adds. The other factor pushing oil higher is winter weather driving up heating oil demand. WTI is up 3.2% at $76.27 a barrel, and Brent is 3% higher at $79.23 after touching highs of $77.86 and $80.75, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1207 GMT - The winter weather that has lifted natural gas prices is extending to oil as concerns about supply from Russia and Iran coincide with higher demand for heating fuels. "Weather roulette has hit the oil patch this morning," with American and European models showing extreme cold for Texas and raising fears of freeze-offs, TP ICAP's Scott Shelton says in a note. While ensemble runs aren't as cold, "the risks are clearly there for Texas with possibly some refinery freezing issues as well," he adds. WTI is up 3.5% at $76.48 a barrel, and Brent is 3.5% higher at $79.62 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1221 GMT - The U.K. has less than a week of gas demand in store due to colder-than-usual weather in the country and the end of Russian gas pipeline supplies through Ukraine on December 31, 2024, Chris O'Shea, chief executive of Centrica, says. As of Thursday, U.K. storage sites were 26% lower than at the same time last year, leaving them around half full, the group says. O'Shea says the U.K. needs to boost its energy storage capacity as it becomes increasingly reliant on renewable sources. The U.K. has some of the lowest levels of gas storage in Europe at 12 days average or 7.5 peak winter days, compared to Germany at 89 days, and France at 103 days, the company notes. Centrica shares are up 0.45% at 1.33 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1641 GMT - Europe in February, why not? Winter is typically a slow time for transatlantic travel, but Delta President Glen Hauenstein says this year has been different. American tourists see their money go farther in Europe right now: "Europe is an incredibly screaming buy as a tourist destination," he says. And with smaller crowds and decent weather--particularly in southern Europe, travelers are finding winter trips have their appeal. Hauenstein says he doesn't believe the new appetite for winter travel will hamper the more popular summer season. "We're seeing robust demand in the off-peak, and I'm sure that the peak is going to be even better," he says. (alison.sider@wsj.com; @alyrose)

1252 GMT - Renault stock is still rated as abuy, but the world is getting more complicated, Citi analysts Harald C Hendrikse and Soumava Banerjee write. Excluding Ferrari, Renault was the best performing stock in European autos in 2024. The French carmaker's management executed well and earnings were boosted by a disciplined sales channel strategy, new model roll-out with improved margins and a lack of China exposure. The company also benefited from much-improved free cash flow and balance sheet. However, risks from worsening macro and EU carbon dioxide regulations suggest the risk-reward has deteriorated, Citi says. Citi closes its short-term upside view and removes Renault from its focus list. It has a 52 euro target price on the stock. Shares trade 0.5% lower at 45.59 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1109 GMT - Delta doesn't see travel demand slowing down in 2025. CEO Ed Bastian notes consumers are increasingly opting for the airline's premium products and experiences, a phenomenon happening across the industry that more airlines are leaning into and prioritizing. A snapshot of this demand came in November and December when Delta saw four of the top 10 revenue days in its history and double-digit growth in cash bookings driven by both leisure and corporate travelers. President Glen Hauenstein says revenue growth in 4Q came in ahead of its guidance as demand trends accelerated through the quarter and expects adjusted revenue in the March quarter to be up to 9% higher than 2024. Delta climbs 5.3% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1203 GMT - Stellantis shares are cheap, but 2025 remains uncertain, Citi analysts Harald C Hendrikse and Soumava Banerjee write. Although the company isn't Citi's favorite pick, it understands why investors may look at it again. Stellantis was the worst performer in European autos last year, but the company's U.S. exposure versus no China exposure, continued strong U.S. market demand and weak sentiment could see the shares trade higher in early 2025 as investors hope for a recovery. "We still prefer other names on lower valuation (Renault, Volkswagen), and Porsche on earnings stabilization." It raises its target price to 13 euros from 12.40 euros, opens a 90-day positive short-term view, and keeps a neutral rating. Shares rise 1.4% to 12.59 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1147 GMT - The U.K. Competition and Markets Authority's investigation into Global Business Travel's planned $570 million bid for CWT Holdings is fatally flawed, a filing from both companies says. A CMA probe found in November that the two companies compete closely for multinational customers and their merger risks reducing choice and raising prices. The companies hit back saying the CMA's definition of their market was "fundamentally erroneous". They said the regulator's interim decision was "fatally undermined by a biased and unrepresentative market investigation," and insisted they faced strong competition from other travel companies as well as hotels and airlines. GBT and CWT offered concessions, including price commitments and selling off some customer contracts to a rival, to get their deal greenlit by CMA's provisional Jan. 26 deadline and to end the probe. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

data.title: Former Swiss finance minister warns about size of enlarged UBS, newspaper says
data.description:
UBS could be seen as being too big for Switzerland following its takeover of Credit Suisse, former Swiss Finance Minister Ueli Maurer said on Saturday, with measures needed to reduce the risks of the enlarged bank.

"If you look at the numbers alone and compare UBS with the Swiss economy, it is too big," Maurer told newspaper Tages-Anzeiger. "Therefore, the risk must be reduced."

At around $1.7 trillion, UBS's balance sheet is double the size of annual Swiss economic output, giving the bank exceptional weight for a major economy.

Should the bank fail, there are no local rivals left to absorb it, while the cost of nationalisation could severely damage public finances, experts have warned.

Reducing risks was primarily the responsibility of shareholders via their choice of board members, Maurer said.

"They must take responsibility, not the taxpayers in the end," said Maurer, who left office months before the final collapse of Credit Suisse in March 2023.

"Legislative measures must also be examined," said Maurer, who also defended himself after a recent parliamentary report raised questions about his actions as the Credit Suisse crisis worsened at the end of 2022.

The Swiss government last year laid out plans for tougher capital requirements for UBS and Switzerland's three other big banks in a bid to make the financial sector more robust after Credit Suisse's demise.

Details of the exact capital requirements are yet to emerge, but the possibility that UBS could be made to hold $15 billion to $25 billion in additional capital has met resistance from the bank.

Maurer said if the capital requirements were too high, Swiss banks would no longer be competitive and may look to be based elsewhere.

"For the Swiss economy with its many international multi-nationals, a large bank is a locational advantage," he said. "But risks must be minimized."

UBS declined to comment on the interview. The bank's CEO Sergio Ermotti earlier this month told Migros Magazine that UBS had enough capital to cover potential problems.

The bank supported many of the Swiss government's proposals to improve banking regulation, but they had to be targetted and proportionate, Ermotti told the magazine.
